title: Saga Pattern Deep Dive
summary: Saga orchestration with state machines, compensation design, idempotency, and implementing Sagas with Axon/Temporal.

sections.content:
## Sagas in Detail

A Saga is a sequence of local transactions where each step either completes successfully or triggers compensating transactions for all previous steps. We covered basics in Distributed Transactions — here we go deep on implementation.

**Key design decisions:**
1. Choreography vs Orchestration (choice of coupling model)
2. How to handle partial failures and compensation
3. Idempotency of all steps
4. State persistence and recovery

sections.title: Saga State Machine — Order Placement
sections.content:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OrderPending: Order Created
    OrderPending --> InventoryReserving: Reserve Inventory
    InventoryReserving --> PaymentProcessing: Inventory Reserved
    InventoryReserving --> OrderCancelled: Inventory Failed
    PaymentProcessing --> OrderConfirmed: Payment Succeeded
    PaymentProcessing --> InventoryReleasing: Payment Failed
    InventoryReleasing --> OrderCancelled: Inventory Released
    OrderConfirmed --> [*]
    OrderCancelled --> [*]

sections.title: Temporal Workflow Execution Model
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Temporal as Temporal Server
    participant Worker
    participant DB as Workflow State
    Client->>Temporal: Start workflow
    Temporal->>DB: Persist workflow start
    Temporal->>Worker: Schedule activity 1
    Worker->>Worker: Execute activity 1
    Worker-->>Temporal: Complete
    Temporal->>DB: Persist progress
    Note over Temporal,DB: Worker crashes here!
    Temporal->>Worker: Re-schedule activity 2 (state recovered)
    Worker->>Worker: Execute activity 2
    Worker-->>Client: Workflow complete

## Common Saga Failure Scenarios

### Counterfactual transactions
A compensating transaction runs but the forward transaction already partially completed:

```
Charge card → SUCCESS (bank charged)
Save payment record → CRASH (DB down)
On retry: duplicate charge attempt!
```

**Solution**: Idempotency key
```java
// Bank API call includes idempotency key
paymentGateway.charge(ChargeRequest.builder()
    .idempotencyKey("order-" + orderId)  // same key on retry
    .amount(amount)
    .build());
// Bank sees same key → returns same result, doesn't double charge
```

### Pivot transaction
The point of no return in a saga — steps before are compensable, steps after are not (e.g., payment charged, shipment created).

sections.question: Why must compensating transactions be idempotent in a Saga?
options: Because compensating transactions are faster when idempotent
Because the saga executor may retry compensating transactions multiple times due to failures, and running the compensation twice must not cause double-compensation
Because idempotent compensations don't need to contact external services
Because Temporal requires idempotency for all workflow steps
sections.explanation: If a compensating transaction fails partway through (network timeout, crash), the saga executor will retry it. If releasing inventory is not idempotent, a double-release could release more inventory than was originally reserved. Every compensation must be safe to call multiple times — tracking 'already compensated' state.
